questions.q: What tense is used to express a general truth or universal fact?
opts: (A) Present Simple
(B) Present Continuous
(C) Past Simple
(D) Future Simple
questions.explanation: Present Simple is used to express general truths or universal facts, e.g., 'Water freezes at 0°C'.

questions.q: Which sentence is in the Passive Voice?
opts: (A) The manager will call the meeting.
(B) The meeting will be called by the manager.
(C) The manager is calling the meeting.
(D) The meeting is being called by the manager.
questions.explanation: The Passive Voice is used to emphasize the action rather than the doer, e.g., 'The meeting will be called by the manager'.

questions.q: What is the difference between 'was' and 'were' in the Past Simple tense?
opts: (A) 'Was' is used for singular subjects and 'were' for plural subjects.
(B) 'Was' is used for plural subjects and 'were' for singular subjects.
(C) 'Was' is used for past actions and 'were' for past states.
(D) 'Was' is used for past states and 'were' for past actions.
questions.explanation: In the Past Simple tense, 'was' is used for singular subjects and 'were' for plural subjects, e.g., 'I was happy' but 'We were happy'.

questions.q: Which of the following sentences is in the Past Perfect tense?
opts: (A) I had eaten breakfast before I went to school.
(B) I eat breakfast before I go to school.
(C) I will eat breakfast before I go to school.
(D) I was eating breakfast before I went to school.
questions.explanation: The Past Perfect tense is used to express an action that happened before another action in the past, e.g., 'I had eaten breakfast before I went to school'.

questions.q: What is the difference between 'can' and 'could' in the Past Simple tense?
opts: (A) 'Can' is used for ability and 'could' for possibility.
(B) 'Can' is used for possibility and 'could' for ability.
(C) 'Can' is used for past actions and 'could' for past states.
(D) 'Can' is used for past states and 'could' for past actions.
questions.explanation: In the Past Simple tense, 'can' is used for ability and 'could' for possibility, e.g., 'I could swim' but 'I can swim' (ability).

questions.q: Which sentence is in the Future Simple tense?
opts: (A) I will be going to the movies tonight.
(B) I will go to the movies tonight.
(C) I am going to the movies tonight.
(D) I was going to the movies last night.
questions.explanation: The Future Simple tense is used to express future actions or events, e.g., 'I will go to the movies tonight'.

questions.q: Which of the following sentences is in the Present Continuous tense?
opts: (A) I am eating breakfast now.
(B) I eat breakfast every morning.
(C) I will eat breakfast tomorrow.
(D) I was eating breakfast yesterday.
questions.explanation: The Present Continuous tense is used to express actions that are happening now, e.g., 'I am eating breakfast now'.

questions.q: What is the difference between 'must' and 'should' in the Present Simple tense?
opts: (A) 'Must' is used for strong recommendations and 'should' for weak recommendations.
(B) 'Must' is used for weak recommendations and 'should' for strong recommendations.
(C) 'Must' is used for ability and 'should' for possibility.
(D) 'Must' is used for possibility and 'should' for ability.
questions.explanation: In the Present Simple tense, 'must' is used for strong recommendations and 'should' for weak recommendations, e.g., 'You must try this' but 'You should try this'.

questions.q: Which sentence is in the Present Perfect tense?
opts: (A) I have eaten breakfast this morning.
(B) I eat breakfast every morning.
(C) I will eat breakfast tomorrow.
(D) I was eating breakfast yesterday.
questions.explanation: The Present Perfect tense is used to express actions that started in the past and continue up to the present, e.g., 'I have eaten breakfast this morning'.

questions.q: What is the difference between 'may' and 'might' in the Past Simple tense?
opts: (A) 'May' is used for permission and 'might' for possibility.
(B) 'May' is used for possibility and 'might' for permission.
(C) 'May' is used for ability and 'might' for possibility.
(D) 'May' is used for possibility and 'might' for ability.
questions.explanation: In the Past Simple tense, 'may' is used for permission and 'might' for possibility, e.g., 'I may go' but 'I might go' (possibility).

questions.q: Which sentence is in the Past Perfect Continuous tense?
opts: (A) I had been eating breakfast for an hour before I went to school.
(B) I eat breakfast every morning.
(C) I will eat breakfast tomorrow.
(D) I was eating breakfast yesterday.
questions.explanation: The Past Perfect Continuous tense is used to express an action that started in the past and continued up to another action in the past, e.g., 'I had been eating breakfast for an hour before I went to school'.

questions.q: What is the difference between 'shall' and 'will' in the Future Simple tense?
opts: (A) 'Shall' is used for first person singular and 'will' for all other persons.
(B) 'Shall' is used for all persons and 'will' for first person singular.
(C) 'Shall' is used for strong recommendations and 'will' for weak recommendations.
(D) 'Shall' is used for weak recommendations and 'will' for strong recommendations.
questions.explanation: In the Future Simple tense, 'shall' is used for first person singular and 'will' for all other persons, e.g., 'I shall go' but 'You will go'.

questions.q: Which sentence is in the Present Perfect Continuous tense?
opts: (A) I have been eating breakfast since 8am.
(B) I eat breakfast every morning.
(C) I will eat breakfast tomorrow.
(D) I was eating breakfast yesterday.
questions.explanation: The Present Perfect Continuous tense is used to express an action that started in the past and continues up to the present, e.g., 'I have been eating breakfast since 8am'.

questions.q: Which of the following sentences is in the Future Perfect tense?
opts: (A) I will have eaten breakfast by 8am.
(B) I eat breakfast every morning.
(C) I will eat breakfast tomorrow.
(D) I was eating breakfast yesterday.
questions.explanation: The Future Perfect tense is used to express an action that will be completed before another action in the future, e.g., 'I will have eaten breakfast by 8am'.

questions.q: Which sentence is in the Past Perfect tense?
opts: (A) I had eaten breakfast before I went to school.
(B) I was eating breakfast before I went to school.
(C) I had been eating breakfast for an hour before I went to school.
(D) I was having breakfast before I went to school.
questions.explanation: The Past Perfect tense is used to express an action that happened before another action in the past, e.g., 'I had eaten breakfast before I went to school'.

questions.q: What is the difference between 'had better' and 'ought to' in the Present Simple tense?
opts: (A) 'Had better' is used for strong recommendations and 'ought to' for weak recommendations.
(B) 'Had better' is used for weak recommendations and 'ought to' for strong recommendations.
(C) 'Had better' is used for ability and 'ought to' for possibility.
(D) 'Had better' is used for possibility and 'ought to' for ability.
questions.explanation: In the Present Simple tense, 'had better' is used for strong recommendations and 'ought to' for weak recommendations, e.g., 'You had better try this' but 'You ought to try this'.

questions.q: Which sentence is in the Future Perfect Continuous tense?
opts: (A) I will have been eating breakfast for an hour by 8am.
(B) I eat breakfast every morning.
(C) I will eat breakfast tomorrow.
(D) I was eating breakfast yesterday.
questions.explanation: The Future Perfect Continuous tense is used to express an action that will be completed before another action in the future, e.g., 'I will have been eating breakfast for an hour by 8am'.

questions.q: What is the difference between 'may have' and 'might have' in the Past Simple tense?
opts: (A) 'May have' is used for permission and 'might have' for possibility.
(B) 'May have' is used for possibility and 'might have' for permission.
(C) 'May have' is used for ability and 'might have' for possibility.
(D) 'May have' is used for possibility and 'might have' for ability.
questions.explanation: In the Past Simple tense, 'may have' is used for permission and 'might have' for possibility, e.g., 'I may have gone' but 'I might have gone' (possibility).

questions.q: Which sentence is in the Present Perfect Passive Voice?
opts: (A) The meeting has been called by the manager.
(B) The meeting is being called by the manager.
(C) The meeting will be called by the manager.
(D) The meeting was called by the manager.
questions.explanation: The Present Perfect Passive Voice is used to express an action that started in the past and continues up to the present, e.g., 'The meeting has been called by the manager'.

questions.q: What is the difference between 'should have' and 'ought to have' in the Present Simple tense?
opts: (A) 'Should have' is used for strong recommendations and 'ought to have' for weak recommendations.
(B) 'Should have' is used for weak recommendations and 'ought to have' for strong recommendations.
(C) 'Should have' is used for ability and 'ought to have' for possibility.
(D) 'Should have' is used for possibility and 'ought to have' for ability.
questions.explanation: In the Present Simple tense, 'should have' is used for strong recommendations and 'ought to have' for weak recommendations, e.g., 'You should have tried this' but 'You ought to have tried this'.
